examples.explanation:
Step 1: Start from step 0. minCost(0) = cost[0] + min(minCost(1), minCost(2)).
Step 2: Compute minCost(1) = cost[1] + min(minCost(2), minCost(3)).
Step 3: Compute minCost(2) = cost[2] + min(minCost(3), minCost(4)).
Step 4: Base case: minCost(3) = 0 (beyond array), minCost(4) = 0.
Step 5: Back-substitute: minCost(2) = 20 + min(0, 0) = 20.
Step 6: minCost(1) = 15 + min(20, 0) = 15 + 0 = 15.
Step 7: minCost(0) = 10 + min(15, 20) = 10 + 15 = 25.
Step 8: Start from step 1: minCost(1) = 15 (already computed).
Step 9: Final answer = min(25, 15) = 15.

Step 1: Start from step 0. minCost(0) = 1 + min(minCost(1), minCost(2)).
Step 2: Recursively compute minCost(1) = 100 + min(minCost(2), minCost(3)).
Step 3: minCost(2) = 1 + min(minCost(3), minCost(4)).
Step 4: Continue until base cases at i=10 (beyond array).
Step 5: Compute all values: minCost(9)=1+min(0,0)=1, minCost(8)=100+min(1,0)=100, minCost(7)=1+min(100,1)=2, minCost(6)=1+min(2,100)=3, minCost(5)=100+min(3,2)=102, minCost(4)=1+min(102,3)=4, minCost(3)=1+min(4,102)=5, minCost(2)=1+min(5,4)=5, minCost(1)=100+min(5,5)=105, minCost(0)=1+min(105,5)=6.
Step 6: Start from step 1: minCost(1)=105.
Step 7: Final answer = min(6, 105) = 6.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all possible paths from each step, calculating the minimum cost by considering both one-step and two-step moves. This approach is straightforward but inefficient due to repeated calculations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Min Cost Climbing Stairs via Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all paths from each step to find minimum cost to reach top.
ladders.explanation: The Min Cost Climbing Stairs problem requires finding the minimum cost to reach the top of a staircase where each step has an associated cost, and you can climb either 1 or 2 steps at a time. The brute force recursion approach solves this by exploring every possible path from the current step to the top. The intuition is that at any step i, the minimum cost to reach the top is the cost of step i plus the minimum of the costs from step i+1 and i+2. This recursive definition naturally captures all possibilities but leads to exponential time complexity due to repeated calculations of the same subproblems. The algorithm starts from step 0 or step 1 (since you can start from either) and recursively computes costs. The base cases are when i is beyond the last step (cost 0) or at the last step (cost of that step). The time complexity is O(2^n) because each call branches into two recursive calls, and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. This approach connects to the parent problem by being the most straightforward but inefficient solution, highlighting the need for optimization techniques like memoization or dynamic programming. Common mistakes include incorrect base cases (e.g., not handling indices beyond the array properly) and off-by-one errors in indexing. Real code pattern: Define a recursive function minCost(i) that returns the minimum cost from step i to the top, with base cases for i >= n, and call min(minCost(0), minCost(1)) for the final answer.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start from step 0. minCost(0) = cost[0] + min(minCost(1), minCost(2)).
Step 2: Compute minCost(1) = cost[1] + min(minCost(2), minCost(3)).
Step 3: Compute minCost(2) = cost[2] + min(minCost(3), minCost(4)).
Step 4: Base case: minCost(3) = 0 (beyond array), minCost(4) = 0.
Step 5: Back-substitute: minCost(2) = 20 + min(0, 0) = 20.
Step 6: minCost(1) = 15 + min(20, 0) = 15 + 0 = 15.
Step 7: minCost(0) = 10 + min(15, 20) = 10 + 15 = 25.
Step 8: Start from step 1: minCost(1) = 15 (already computed).
Step 9: Final answer = min(25, 15) = 15.

Step 1: Start from step 0. minCost(0) = 1 + min(minCost(1), minCost(2)).
Step 2: Recursively compute minCost(1) = 100 + min(minCost(2), minCost(3)).
Step 3: minCost(2) = 1 + min(minCost(3), minCost(4)).
Step 4: Continue until base cases at i=10 (beyond array).
Step 5: Compute all values: minCost(9)=1+min(0,0)=1, minCost(8)=100+min(1,0)=100, minCost(7)=1+min(100,1)=2, minCost(6)=1+min(2,100)=3, minCost(5)=100+min(3,2)=102, minCost(4)=1+min(102,3)=4, minCost(3)=1+min(4,102)=5, minCost(2)=1+min(5,4)=5, minCost(1)=100+min(5,5)=105, minCost(0)=1+min(105,5)=6.
Step 6: Start from step 1: minCost(1)=105.
Step 7: Final answer = min(6, 105) = 6.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function to Compute Minimum Cost from a Given Step
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that calculates minimum cost from step i to the top.
ladders.explanation: The key sub-routine in the brute force approach is the recursive function minCost(i), which computes the minimum cost to reach the top starting from step i. The intuition is that at step i, you have two choices: climb 1 step to i+1 or 2 steps to i+2. The minimum cost is the cost of step i plus the minimum of the costs from i+1 and i+2. This recursive definition ensures all paths are explored. The algorithm: Define function minCost(i, costArray, n) where n is length of costArray. Base cases: if i >= n, return 0 (no cost beyond the staircase); if i == n-1, return costArray[i] (last step cost). Recursive case: return costArray[i] + min(minCost(i+1), minCost(i+2)). Time complexity is O(2^n) due to exponential branching, and space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This sub-routine feeds into the full problem by being called from steps 0 and 1 to find the overall minimum. Common mistakes include forgetting base cases for i >= n or incorrectly handling the last step. Real code pattern: def minCost(i, cost): if i >= len(cost): return 0; if i == len(cost)-1: return cost[i]; return cost[i] + min(minCost(i+1, cost), minCost(i+2, cost)).

examples.trace:
Step 1: minCost(0) = cost[0] + min(minCost(1), minCost(2)).
Step 2: Compute minCost(1) = 15 + min(minCost(2), minCost(3)).
Step 3: Compute minCost(2) = 20 + min(minCost(3), minCost(4)).
Step 4: Base cases: minCost(3)=0, minCost(4)=0.
Step 5: minCost(2) = 20 + min(0,0) = 20.
Step 6: minCost(1) = 15 + min(20,0) = 15 + 0 = 15.
Step 7: minCost(0) = 10 + min(15,20) = 10 + 15 = 25.

Step 1: minCost(1) = cost[1] + min(minCost(2), minCost(3)).
Step 2: Compute minCost(2) = 1 + min(minCost(3), minCost(4)).
Step 3: Base cases: minCost(3)=0, minCost(4)=0.
Step 4: minCost(2) = 1 + min(0,0) = 1.
Step 5: minCost(1) = 100 + min(1,0) = 100 + 0 = 100.
Step 6: Correction: min(1,0)=0, so minCost(1)=100+0=100. Wait, re-check: minCost(1) should be cost[1] + min(minCost(2), minCost(3)) = 100 + min(1,0) = 100 + 0 = 100. But expected output is 101? Let's recalc: Actually, minCost(2)=1, minCost(3)=0, so min(1,0)=0, thus 100+0=100. But problem might expect 101 if starting from step 1? For sub-routine, output is min cost from step 1 to top: paths: step1->step2->top: cost=100+1=101, step1->step3: cost=100+0=100, min is 100. So output should be 100. Adjust trace: Step 5: minCost(1) = 100 + min(1,0) = 100 + 0 = 100. Output 100.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Decision Making at Each Step
ladders.desc: Understand the recursive choice between one-step and two-step moves.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force approach is the recursive decision-making process at each step: choosing between a one-step move (to i+1) or a two-step move (to i+2) to minimize cost. The intuition is that the problem has optimal substructure—the minimum cost from step i depends on the minimum costs from steps i+1 and i+2. This is a classic example of a recursive divide-and-conquer strategy where the problem is broken down into smaller subproblems. The algorithm involves evaluating two recursive calls at each step and taking the minimum. Time complexity arises from the binary tree of recursive calls, leading to O(2^n), and space complexity is O(n) due to the call stack. This logic feeds into the key sub-routine by defining the recursive case. Common mistakes include not considering both moves or incorrect base case handling. Real code pattern: At step i, compute costOneStep = cost[i] + minCost(i+1), costTwoStep = cost[i] + minCost(i+2), and return min(costOneStep, costTwoStep).

examples.trace:
Step 1: At step 0, options: move to step 1 or step 2.
Step 2: Compute cost to step 1: 10 + minCost(1). minCost(1) = 15 + min(minCost(2), minCost(3)).
Step 3: minCost(2) = 20 + min(minCost(3), minCost(4)) = 20 + min(0,0) = 20.
Step 4: minCost(1) = 15 + min(20,0) = 15 + 0 = 15.
Step 5: Cost to step 1 = 10 + 15 = 25.
Step 6: Compute cost to step 2: 10 + minCost(2) = 10 + 20 = 30.
Step 7: Minimum at step 0 = min(25, 30) = 25.

Step 1: At step 1, options: move to step 2 or step 3.
Step 2: Compute cost to step 2: 100 + minCost(2). minCost(2) = 1 + min(minCost(3), minCost(4)) = 1 + min(0,0) = 1.
Step 3: Cost to step 2 = 100 + 1 = 101.
Step 4: Compute cost to step 3: 100 + minCost(3) = 100 + 0 = 100.
Step 5: Minimum at step 1 = min(101, 100) = 100.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Function Calls with Base Cases
ladders.desc: Learn to implement recursive functions with proper base cases for termination.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on creating recursive functions that include base cases to prevent infinite recursion. In the context of Min Cost Climbing Stairs, the base cases are when the step index i is beyond the staircase (i >= n) or at the last step (i == n-1). The intuition is that recursion must have stopping conditions; otherwise, it would call itself indefinitely. For this problem, when i >= n, the cost is 0 because you've reached or passed the top. When i == n-1, you must pay the cost of that last step to reach the top. The algorithm involves checking these conditions before making recursive calls. Time and space analysis are not primary here, but in practice, each call adds to the stack. This concept feeds into the core logic by ensuring the recursive decisions terminate correctly. Common mistakes include missing base cases or incorrect conditions (e.g., using i > n instead of i >= n). Real code pattern: def recursiveCost(i, cost): if i >= len(cost): return 0; if i == len(cost)-1: return cost[i]; # then recursive calls.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Call minCost(3) with cost array length 3.
Step 2: Check base case: i=3 >= len(cost)=3, so return 0.
Step 3: No recursive calls made.

Step 1: Call minCost(2) with cost array length 3.
Step 2: Check base cases: i=2 is not >= 3, but i=2 == len(cost)-1=2, so return cost[2]=20.
Step 3: No recursive calls made.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Array Elements and Integer Addition
ladders.desc: Perform fundamental operations: access cost array and add integers.
ladders.explanation: The basic operations in the brute force approach involve accessing elements from the cost array and performing integer addition. The intuition is that at each step, we need to retrieve the cost associated with that step from the array and add it to the recursive costs from subsequent steps. This is a simple but essential part of the algorithm. The algorithm includes using an index i to access cost[i], and then adding this value to other integers (e.g., results from recursive calls). Time and space analysis are trivial here, but these operations are performed repeatedly in the recursion. This feeds into the building block by being used in the recursive function body. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors or incorrect addition. Real code pattern: currentCost = cost[i]; totalCost = currentCost + min(recursiveCall1, recursiveCall2).

examples.output: Access cost[0]=10, then add to min(recursive results)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Given cost array [10,15,20] and index i=0.
Step 2: Access cost[0]: value is 10.
Step 3: Suppose recursive calls return values A and B.
Step 4: Compute 10 + min(A, B). For example, if A=15 and B=20, then 10 + min(15,20) = 10 + 15 = 25.

examples.output: Access cost[1]=100, then add to min(recursive results)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Given cost array [1,100,1] and index i=1.
Step 2: Access cost[1]: value is 100.
Step 3: Suppose recursive calls return values C and D.
Step 4: Compute 100 + min(C, D). For example, if C=1 and D=0, then 100 + min(1,0) = 100 + 0 = 100.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn the basics of arrays and how to access elements using indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers arrays, which are data structures that store a collection of elements, and indexing, which is the method to access individual elements using their position (index). In the Min Cost Climbing Stairs problem, the cost array holds the cost for each step, and we use indices (like 0, 1, 2, etc.) to retrieve these costs. The intuition is that arrays allow efficient storage and access of related data. For example, cost[0] gives the cost of the first step. The algorithm involves declaring an array, populating it with values, and then using indices in expressions. Time and space analysis are not applicable here, but understanding this is crucial for the basic operations. This feeds into level 4 by enabling array access. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (arrays typically start at index 0) or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real code pattern: cost = [10, 15, 20]; firstCost = cost[0]; // firstCost is 10.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Define array cost with values [10, 15, 20].
Step 2: Indices: 0 -> 10, 1 -> 15, 2 -> 20.
Step 3: Access cost[0]: return the value at index 0, which is 10.

Step 1: Define array cost with values [1, 100, 1].
Step 2: Indices: 0 -> 1, 1 -> 100, 2 -> 1.
Step 3: Access cost[2]: return the value at index 2, which is 1.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Dynamic Programming with Memoization O(n)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to store results of subproblems, avoiding repeated calculations by caching the minimum cost to reach each step. This improves efficiency over brute force while maintaining a top-down structure.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Min Cost Climbing Stairs with Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursion with memoization to find minimum cost to reach top of stairs.
ladders.explanation: This problem asks us to find the minimum cost to reach the top of a staircase where each step has an associated cost, and we can climb either 1 or 2 steps at a time. The intuition behind the memoization approach is that we can break down the problem into overlapping subproblems: the minimum cost to reach step i depends on the minimum cost to reach steps i-1 and i-2. By caching these results, we avoid exponential recomputation. The algorithm works recursively: for any step i, the minimum cost to reach it is cost[i] + min(minCost(i-1), minCost(i-2)). We memoize results in an array to ensure each subproblem is solved only once. The base cases are steps 0 and 1, where the cost is simply cost[0] and cost[1] respectively. The final answer is the minimum of reaching the last step or the step before it, since we can reach the top from either. Time complexity is O(n) because each of n steps is computed once, and space complexity is O(n) for the memoization array plus recursion stack. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete recursive function with memoization. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle base cases properly, not initializing memo array correctly, or incorrectly calculating the final answer. Real code pattern: define recursive function minCost(i) that returns minimum cost to reach step i, with memoization using an array.

examples.trace: Step 0: cost[0] = 10, minCost(0) = 10 (base case). Step 1: cost[1] = 15, minCost(1) = 15 (base case). Step 2: minCost(2) = cost[2] + min(minCost(1), minCost(0)) = 20 + min(15, 10) = 20 + 10 = 30. Final answer: min(minCost(2), minCost(1)) = min(30, 15) = 15.

Step 0: minCost(0) = 1. Step 1: minCost(1) = 100. Step 2: minCost(2) = 1 + min(100, 1) = 2. Step 3: minCost(3) = 1 + min(2, 100) = 3. Step 4: minCost(4) = 1 + min(3, 2) = 3. Step 5: minCost(5) = 100 + min(3, 3) = 103. Step 6: minCost(6) = 1 + min(103, 3) = 4. Step 7: minCost(7) = 1 + min(4, 103) = 5. Step 8: minCost(8) = 100 + min(5, 4) = 104. Step 9: minCost(9) = 1 + min(104, 5) = 6. Final answer: min(minCost(9), minCost(8)) = min(6, 104) = 6.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function with Memoization
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that computes minimum cost for each step with caching.
ladders.explanation: The key sub-routine is a recursive function minCost(i) that returns the minimum cost to reach step i, using memoization to store results. The intuition is that by caching computed values, we transform an exponential recursive solution into a linear one. The algorithm: if i < 0, return 0 (invalid step). If i == 0 or i == 1, return cost[i] (base cases). Otherwise, if memo[i] is not set, compute memo[i] = cost[i] + min(minCost(i-1), minCost(i-2)). Return memo[i]. Time complexity is O(n) as each step is computed once, and space is O(n) for memo array. This feeds into L0 by being the core function called to solve the full problem. Common mistakes include not checking for negative i, incorrect base cases, or not properly initializing memo array. Real code pattern: define array memo of size n initialized to -1, and function minCost(i) that uses memoization.

examples.trace: Call minCost(2): memo[2] not set. Compute minCost(1) = 15 (base case). Compute minCost(0) = 10 (base case). memo[2] = cost[2] + min(15, 10) = 20 + 10 = 30. Return 30.
Call minCost(2): memo[2] not set. Compute minCost(1) = 100 (base case). Compute minCost(0) = 1 (base case). memo[2] = cost[2] + min(100, 1) = 1 + 1 = 2. Return 2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Optimal Substructure and Overlapping Subproblems
ladders.desc: Understand the recurrence relation and why memoization works.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is based on two principles: optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems. Optimal substructure means the optimal solution to the problem can be constructed from optimal solutions to its subproblems. Here, the minimum cost to reach step i is cost[i] plus the minimum of reaching step i-1 or i-2. Overlapping subproblems occur because computing minCost(i) requires minCost(i-1) and minCost(i-2), which themselves require earlier steps, leading to repeated calculations. Memoization works by storing these results to avoid recomputation. The recurrence relation is: minCost(i) = cost[i] + min(minCost(i-1), minCost(i-2)) for i >= 2, with base cases minCost(0)=cost[0], minCost(1)=cost[1]. Time analysis: without memoization, this is exponential O(2^n); with memoization, it becomes O(n). Space is O(n) for storage. This connects to L1 by providing the mathematical foundation for the recursive function. Common mistakes include misdefining the recurrence or not recognizing overlapping subproblems. Real code pattern: understanding that the problem can be broken down recursively with caching.

examples.trace: For step 2: minCost(2) = c + min(minCost(1), minCost(0)) = c + min(b, a). This shows optimal substructure (using subproblems) and overlapping if computed multiple times.

minCost(3) = 4 + min(minCost(2), minCost(1)). minCost(2) = 3 + min(minCost(1), minCost(0)) = 3 + min(2, 1) = 4. minCost(1) = 2. So minCost(3) = 4 + min(4, 2) = 6. Overlapping occurs as minCost(1) is used multiple times.

ladders.title: Building Block — Memoization Technique
ladders.desc: Learn how to implement caching for recursive functions.
ladders.explanation: Memoization is a technique to optimize recursive algorithms by storing the results of expensive function calls and returning the cached result when the same inputs occur again. The intuition is to avoid redundant computations in problems with overlapping subproblems. The algorithm involves: 1) Define a data structure (e.g., array or dictionary) to store computed values. 2) In the recursive function, before computing, check if the result for the current input is already cached. 3) If cached, return it. 4) If not, compute the result, store it in the cache, and return it. Time analysis: reduces time complexity from exponential to linear in many cases. Space complexity is O(n) for the cache. This connects to L2 by providing the implementation method for handling overlapping subproblems. Common mistakes include not initializing cache properly, using wrong data structures, or forgetting to check cache before computation. Real code pattern: use an array memo where memo[i] stores minCost(i), initialized to -1 to indicate not computed.

examples.trace: fib(5) calls fib(4) and fib(3). fib(4) calls fib(3) and fib(2), leading to repeated calls like fib(3) computed multiple times.
fib(5): compute fib(4) (caches result), fib(3) (caches result). When fib(3) is needed again, return cached value instead of recomputing.

testCases.input: Function f(n)=f(n-1)+f(n-2) with memoization for n=5
Compute factorial recursively with memoization for n=4
Memoization for recursive sum of array

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Recursive Function Calls
ladders.desc: Understand how recursive functions work with base cases and recursive cases.
ladders.explanation: Recursive function calls involve a function that calls itself to solve smaller instances of the same problem. The intuition is to break down a problem into simpler subproblems until a base case is reached. The algorithm requires: 1) Base case(s): simple instances that can be solved directly without recursion. 2) Recursive case: where the function calls itself with modified parameters to approach the base case. Time analysis depends on the number of recursive calls; without optimization, it can be exponential. Space complexity includes the call stack, which can be O(n) in depth. This connects to L3 as recursion is the foundation for memoization. Common mistakes include missing base cases (leading to infinite recursion), incorrect recursive calls, or not reducing problem size. Real code pattern: define function f(n) that returns a value based on f(n-1) or f(n-2), with base cases for n=0 or n=1.

examples.trace: fact(3): call fact(2). fact(2): call fact(1). fact(1): return 1 (base case). fact(2): return 2 * 1 = 2. fact(3): return 3 * 2 = 6.
fib(3): call fib(2) and fib(1). fib(2): call fib(1) and fib(0). fib(1): return 1. fib(0): return 0. fib(2): return 1 + 0 = 1. fib(3): return 1 + 1 = 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn how to use arrays to store and access data by index.
ladders.explanation: Arrays are fundamental data structures that store elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible by an index. The intuition is that they allow efficient access to elements based on their position. Key operations: 1) Declaration: create an array of a specific size. 2) Indexing: access or modify an element using its index (starting from 0). 3) Iteration: loop through elements using indices. Time analysis: access by index is O(1), iteration is O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for storing n elements. This connects to L4 as arrays are used to store costs and memoization results. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors, misunderstanding zero-based indexing, or incorrect array initialization. Real code pattern: in Python, use lists like cost = [10, 15, 20]; access cost[0] returns 10.

examples.trace: Index 0: 5. Index 1: 10. Index 2: 15. Modify index 1 to 20: array becomes [5, 20, 15].
Initialize sum=0. i=0: sum+=1, sum=1. i=1: sum+=2, sum=3. i=2: sum+=3, sum=6. Final sum=6.

testCases.input: Create array of size 3 with zeros

approaches.name: Approach 3: Dynamic Programming Bottom-Up O(n)
approaches.summary: Iteratively compute the minimum cost from the base cases upward using an array, where each step's cost depends on the previous two steps. This is efficient and avoids recursion overhead.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Min Cost Climbing Stairs with Bottom-Up DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using iterative DP to find minimum cost to reach top of stairs.
ladders.explanation: This problem asks us to find the minimum cost to reach the top of a staircase where each step has an associated cost, and we can start from either step 0 or step 1, then climb 1 or 2 steps at a time. The bottom-up DP approach builds the solution iteratively from base cases to the final answer. The intuition is that at each step i, the minimum cost to reach step i is the cost of step i plus the minimum of reaching step i-1 or i-2, since we can come from either of those previous steps. We create a DP array where dp[i] represents the minimum cost to reach step i. The base cases are dp[0] = cost[0] and dp[1] = cost[1] since we can start at either step. For i from 2 to n-1, we compute dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]). The final answer is min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2]) because we can reach the top from either of the last two steps. This approach is O(n) time and O(n) space. Connection to L1: This uses the key recurrence relation repeatedly. Common mistakes include incorrect base cases, off-by-one errors with array indices, and forgetting to take min of last two steps for final answer. Real code pattern: def minCostClimbingStairs(cost): n = len(cost); if n <= 1: return 0; dp = [0]*n; dp[0] = cost[0]; dp[1] = cost[1]; for i in range(2, n): dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]); return min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2]).

examples.trace: n=3. dp[0]=10, dp[1]=15. i=2: dp[2]=20+min(15,10)=20+10=30. Final: min(dp[2],dp[1])=min(30,15)=15. Explanation: Start at step 1 (cost 15), jump 2 steps to top (total 15).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Recurrence Relation Computation
ladders.desc: Compute dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]) for a given index i.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the bottom-up DP approach. It computes the minimum cost to reach step i based on the costs to reach previous steps. The intuition is optimal substructure: the optimal solution to reach step i depends on optimal solutions to reach steps i-1 and i-2. At each step, we add the current step's cost to the cheaper of reaching from i-1 or i-2. The algorithm: Given cost array, dp array with dp[0] and dp[1] initialized, for i from 2 to n-1, compute dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]). Time O(1) per step, overall O(n) when used in loop. Space O(1) extra per step. Connection to L0: This is repeated in a loop to build the full DP array. Common mistakes: Using cost[i-1] or cost[i-2] instead of dp values, incorrect index bounds. Real code pattern: for i in range(2, n): dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Optimal Substructure for Stair Climbing
ladders.desc: Understanding that min cost to reach step i depends on min costs to reach i-1 and i-2.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the fundamental insight that enables DP. It states that the minimum cost to reach step i can be expressed in terms of minimum costs to reach previous steps. Specifically, minCost(i) = cost[i] + min(minCost(i-1), minCost(i-2)). The intuition is that to get to step i, you must come from either step i-1 (by taking 1 step) or step i-2 (by taking 2 steps). Since we want the minimum total cost, we choose the cheaper of these two options and add the cost of step i. This property holds because the problem has overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure. The algorithm: Define recurrence relation. Time and space analysis depends on implementation but the logic itself is O(1). Connection to L1: This recurrence is computed in the sub-routine. Common mistakes: Not including cost[i] in the sum, misunderstanding that we can start from step 0 or 1. Real code pattern: Conceptual: minCost[i] = cost[i] + min(minCost[i-1], minCost[i-2]).

examples.input: Step i=2 with cost[2]=20, minCost(0)=10, minCost(1)=15
examples.trace: To reach step 2, options: From step 1 (cost 15) + step 2 cost 20 = 35, or from step 0 (cost 10) + step 2 cost 20 = 30. Min is 30.

examples.input: Step i=3 with cost[3]=1, minCost(1)=100, minCost(2)=2
examples.trace: To reach step 3, options: From step 2 (cost 2) + step 3 cost 1 = 3, or from step 1 (cost 100) + step 3 cost 1 = 101. Min is 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Iteration with Index Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array while accessing elements at relative indices (i-1, i-2).
ladders.explanation: This building block involves looping through an array and performing operations that reference elements at positions relative to the current index. It's essential for implementing the DP recurrence. The intuition is that we need to compute each element based on previous computed elements, so we iterate sequentially from start to end. The algorithm: Initialize an array dp of same length as cost. Set dp[0] and dp[1] as base cases. For i from 2 to n-1, compute dp[i] using dp[i-1] and dp[i-2]. Time O(n) for the loop, space O(n) for the array. Connection to L2: This iteration applies the recurrence relation across all steps. Common mistakes: Starting loop at i=1 or i=0 causing index errors, not handling small arrays (n<2). Real code pattern: n = len(cost); dp = [0]*n; dp[0] = cost[0]; dp[1] = cost[1]; for i in range(2, n): # use dp[i-1] and dp[i-2].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Minimum of Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Compute the smaller of two given numbers.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is fundamental to the DP recurrence, where we need to choose the cheaper of two previous steps. The intuition is simple comparison: given two numbers a and b, we want the minimum value. The algorithm: Use a comparison operator. In pseudocode: min(a, b) returns a if a <= b, else b. Time O(1), space O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used within the array iteration to decide between dp[i-1] and dp[i-2]. Common mistakes: Using max instead of min, incorrect handling of equal values (min should work fine with equality). Real code pattern: min_value = a if a < b else b.

examples.trace: Compare 10 and 15. 10 < 15, so min is 10.
Compare 5 and 5. They are equal, so min is 5 (can return either).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding how to work with arrays. An array is a collection of elements stored at contiguous memory locations, accessible via indices starting from 0. The intuition is that each element has a position, and we can retrieve or modify it using its index. The algorithm: Given an array arr and index i, access arr[i]. Time O(1) for access, space O(n) for the array. Connection to L4: This is needed to get cost values and dp values in the minimum operation. Common mistakes: Using indices out of bounds (e.g., negative or >= length), confusing 0-based indexing. Real code pattern: element = arr[index].

examples.trace: Array has elements at indices 0,1,2. Index 1 refers to the second element, which is 20.
Array has elements at indices 0,1,2,3. Index 3 refers to the fourth element, which is 35.

approaches.name: Approach 4: Dynamic Programming Space-Optimized O(n)
approaches.summary: Optimize space by using only two variables to track the costs of the last two steps, iteratively updating them to compute the minimum cost. This reduces space usage while maintaining linear time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Min Cost Climbing Stairs with Space-Optimized DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using two variables to track minimum costs iteratively.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete Min Cost Climbing Stairs problem solved using space-optimized dynamic programming. The problem asks: given an array cost where cost[i] is the cost to step on stair i, find the minimum cost to reach the top (beyond the last stair). You can start from stair 0 or stair 1, and each step can be 1 or 2 stairs. The space-optimized approach uses only two variables (prev1 and prev2) instead of a full DP array, reducing space from O(n) to O(1) while maintaining O(n) time. Intuition: At each stair i, the minimum cost to reach it is cost[i] plus the minimum of the costs to reach the previous two stairs. By iterating through stairs, we update two variables representing the minimum costs for the last two positions. The final answer is the minimum of the last two computed values, since we can reach the top from either of the last two stairs. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize prev1 = cost[0], prev2 = cost[1] (if n>=2). 2. For i from 2 to n-1: compute current = cost[i] + min(prev1, prev2), then update prev1 = prev2, prev2 = current. 3. Return min(prev1, prev2). Time: O(n) for single pass. Space: O(1) for two variables. Connection to L1: This uses the iterative update subroutine from L1 as its core loop. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle n<2 cases, incorrectly initializing variables, or not updating variables in correct order. Real code pattern: def minCostClimbingStairs(cost): n = len(cost); if n == 1: return cost[0]; prev1, prev2 = cost[0], cost[1]; for i in range(2, n): current = cost[i] + min(prev1, prev2); prev1, prev2 = prev2, current; return min(prev1, prev2).

examples.trace: n=3. Initialize: prev1=10, prev2=15. i=2: cost[2]=20, min(prev1,prev2)=min(10,15)=10, current=20+10=30. Update: prev1=15, prev2=30. Loop ends. Return min(prev1,prev2)=min(15,30)=15. Explanation: Start from stair 1 (cost 15), jump 2 stairs to top (cost 0) = total 15.

n=10. Initialize: prev1=1, prev2=100. i=2: current=1+min(1,100)=2, prev1=100, prev2=2. i=3: current=1+min(100,2)=3, prev1=2, prev2=3. i=4: current=1+min(2,3)=3, prev1=3, prev2=3. i=5: current=100+min(3,3)=103, prev1=3, prev2=103. i=6: current=1+min(3,103)=4, prev1=103, prev2=4. i=7: current=1+min(103,4)=5, prev1=4, prev2=5. i=8: current=100+min(4,5)=104, prev1=5, prev2=104. i=9: current=1+min(5,104)=6, prev1=104, prev2=6. Return min(104,6)=6. Path: 0->2->4->6->7->9->top (costs: 1+1+1+1+1+1=6).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative DP Update with Two Variables
ladders.desc: The loop that updates two variables to compute minimum costs sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core iterative process of the space-optimized DP approach. Given an array cost and initial values for the minimum costs of the first two stairs, it iterates through the remaining stairs, at each step computing the minimum cost to reach the current stair and updating two variables that track the costs for the last two positions. Intuition: In DP, the recurrence is dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]). Instead of storing all dp values, we only need the last two (prev1 for i-2, prev2 for i-1). At each iteration, we compute current for i, then shift: prev1 becomes the old prev2, and prev2 becomes current. This sliding window of two variables efficiently computes the sequence. Step-by-step: Input: cost array, prev1, prev2 initialized. For i from 2 to n-1: current = cost[i] + min(prev1, prev2); prev1 = prev2; prev2 = current. After loop, prev1 and prev2 hold dp[n-2] and dp[n-1]. Time: O(n) for the loop. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is the main loop in the full solution. Common mistakes: Updating prev1 and prev2 in wrong order (must save prev2 before updating), or not using min correctly. Real code pattern: for i in range(2, n): current = cost[i] + min(prev1, prev2); prev1, prev2 = prev2, current. This is a classic pattern for space-optimized DP with dependency on two previous states.

examples.trace: n=4. Start: prev1=10, prev2=15. i=2: cost[2]=20, min(10,15)=10, current=30. Update: prev1=15, prev2=30. i=3: cost[3]=25, min(15,30)=15, current=40. Update: prev1=30, prev2=40. Loop ends. Final prev1=30, prev2=40. These represent dp[2] and dp[3].

n=5. Start: prev1=1, prev2=2. i=2: cost[2]=3, min(1,2)=1, current=4, update to prev1=2, prev2=4. i=3: cost[3]=4, min(2,4)=2, current=6, update to prev1=4, prev2=6. i=4: cost[4]=5, min(4,6)=4, current=9, update to prev1=6, prev2=9. Final prev1=6, prev2=9.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Recurrence Relation for Minimum Cost
ladders.desc: The mathematical relation: min cost at step i = cost[i] + min(cost to reach i-1, cost to reach i-2).
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental recurrence relation that drives the DP solution. For each stair i (0-indexed), let dp[i] be the minimum cost to reach and step on stair i. The relation is: dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]) for i >= 2, with base cases dp[0] = cost[0] and dp[1] = cost[1]. Intuition: To reach stair i, you must come from either stair i-1 (with cost dp[i-1]) or stair i-2 (with cost dp[i-2]), and then pay cost[i] to step on i. Choosing the minimum of the two previous costs ensures optimality. This relation exhibits optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems, hallmark of DP. Step-by-step: Define dp array of size n. Set dp[0]=cost[0], dp[1]=cost[1]. For i from 2 to n-1: dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]). Finally, the answer is min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2]) since you can reach the top from the last or second-last stair. Time: O(n) to fill dp array. Space: O(n) for dp array. Connection to L1: This recurrence is computed iteratively in L1 using two variables instead of full array. Common mistakes: Forgetting base cases, incorrect indexing, or not considering the final min. Real code pattern: dp = [0]*n; dp[0]=cost[0]; dp[1]=cost[1]; for i in range(2,n): dp[i]=cost[i]+min(dp[i-1],dp[i-2]); return min(dp[n-1],dp[n-2]). This is the unoptimized DP version.

ladders.title: Building Block — Minimum of Two Values in a Sequence
ladders.desc: Computing the minimum between two consecutive values in a DP context.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves taking two values (representing costs to reach previous stairs) and selecting the smaller one to use in a recurrence. In the DP relation, at each step i, we need min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]). This operation is crucial because it embodies the choice: from which previous stair should we come to minimize total cost? Intuition: Since you can jump 1 or 2 stairs, you have two options at each step; picking the cheaper option ensures the path remains optimal. This is a greedy choice within the DP framework. Step-by-step: Given two numbers a and b (which could be dp values, costs, or other), compute min(a,b). In the algorithm, this is done at each iteration. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This min operation is part of the recurrence relation dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]). Common mistakes: Using max instead of min, or not updating the values correctly for the next iteration. Real code pattern: min_value = min(prev1, prev2). This simple operation is repeated in a loop to build the solution.

examples.trace: Compute min(10,15). Compare: 10 < 15, so min is 10.

examples.input: Sequence: prev1=3, prev2=5, then update to prev1=5, prev2=8, compute min again
examples.trace: First: min(3,5)=3. After update, prev1=5, prev2=8. Second: min(5,8)=5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through an Array with Index
ladders.desc: Looping through an array from start to end, accessing each element by index.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves traversing an array sequentially using an index variable. In the DP solution, we iterate from i=2 to n-1 to compute costs for each stair. Intuition: Arrays are ordered collections; processing each element in order allows us to build up a solution incrementally. Step-by-step: Initialize an index i (e.g., i=2). While i is less than array length n, access element cost[i], perform operations, then increment i (e.g., i+=1). This ensures each element is visited exactly once. Time: O(n) for full traversal. Space: O(1) for index variable. Connection to L3: This iteration provides the sequence in which we apply the min operation and updates. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors (e.g., starting at i=1 or going to i=n), or not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: for i in range(2, len(cost)): # use cost[i]. This is a basic for-loop construct.

examples.trace: n=4. i=2: access cost[2]=20. i=3: access cost[3]=25. i=4 stops.
n=5. i=2: cost[2]=3. i=3: cost[3]=4. i=4: cost[4]=5. i=5 stops.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Array Indexing and Access
ladders.desc: Accessing elements in an array using zero-based indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: how to get a value from an array given its position. An array is a collection of items stored contiguously. Each item has an index starting from 0. To access an element, use the syntax array[index]. Intuition: Think of an array as a row of boxes numbered 0,1,2,...; indexing lets you pick a specific box. Step-by-step: Given an array A and an integer i (0 <= i < length of A), A[i] returns the value at position i. For example, if A = [10,20,30], then A[0]=10, A[1]=20, A[2]=30. Time: O(1) for direct access. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This basic access is used in the iteration to get cost[i] at each step. Common mistakes: Using 1-based indexing (common in math but not programming), accessing out of bounds (e.g., i >= length), or confusing index with value. Real code pattern: value = cost[i]. This is fundamental to all array processing.

examples.trace: Array has indices 0,1,2. Index 1 corresponds to the second element: value 10.
Index 0: first element = 100. Index 3: fourth element = 400.
